First, participants may form rapid, largely implicit evaluations of stimulus authenticity upon initial exposure, which subsequently shape their arousal responses. 
Under this account, when an image labelled as AI-generated is nonetheless perceived as photograph-like, it may be experienced as more realistic and therefore more arousing, with this evaluation later reflected in Phase 2 realism ratings. 
In this sense, arousal ratings would be guided by participants' initial impressions of authenticity, even if these impressions are not explicitly remembered or consciously endorsed. 
An alternative interpretation is that the direction of influence is reversed: rather than authenticity judgments driving arousal, some images may simply elicit stronger or weaker affective responses independent of their assigned label, and participants may retrospectively align their realism judgments with these affective reactions, such that less arousing images are more likely to be classified as AI-generated, and more arousing images as photographs. 
In this case, perceived authenticity would be inferred from the emotional response to the stimulus, rather than determining it. 
Together, these interpretations suggest that arousal and perceived authenticity may be reciprocally related, with the observed congruency effects reflecting either the influence of initial authenticity impressions on affective response, or post hoc alignment of realism judgments with experienced arousal.

While the present data cannot adjudicate between these accounts, one factor that may help explain variability in the strength of this effect is interoceptive ability. 
Theoretical accounts of perceptual reality monitoring propose that interoceptive precision shapes whether perceptual content is attributed to external reality, with disrupted interoception implicated in reality-monitoring failures in clinical populations [@dijkstra2024disrupted]. 
We propose that an analogous mechanism may operate in the healthy range: participants who are more attuned to their own bodily arousal may place correspondingly more weight on that signal when judging whether an image is a genuine photograph, producing a stronger crossover effect, whereas participants with lower interoceptive accuracy may rely more heavily on contextual cues such as the assigned label. 
This account could first be tested correlationally, by incorporating a standard measure of interoceptive accuracy (e.g., a heartbeat discrimination task) alongside the existing paradigm and examining whether interoceptive accuracy predicts the size of the congruency effect. 
However, any such association could equally reflect a shared underlying trait, such as general emotional sensitivity, rather than a causal contribution of interoception specifically, and so would not on its own establish that interoceptive accuracy drives the effect.

A more direct test would be to manipulate interoceptive accuracy experimentally. 
Transcutaneous vagus nerve stimulation (tVNS) offers one way to do this: the vagus nerve is the primary pathway conveying internal bodily signals to the brain, and tVNS has been proposed as a tool for manipulating interoceptive processing at multiple levels [@paciorek2020vagus], with one study showing that tVNS improved participants' accuracy in identifying their own heartbeats relative to sham stimulation, although it did not improve interoceptive awareness specifically [@villani2019transcutaneous]. 
A future study could randomly assign participants to active or sham tVNS while completing the existing two-phase paradigm, and test whether active stimulation increases the size of the crossover effect relative to sham. 
A larger crossover effect under active tVNS would support the conclusion that interoceptive accuracy contributes causally to the alignment between felt arousal and realism judgements, whereas no difference between conditions would suggest that any correlational link observed in the first step reflects some other shared factor rather than interoception itself.

Notably, this study is not without limitations. 
Firstly, as expressed above, a substantial proportion of participants did not believe the labels were accurate (42% in Study 1 and 32% in Study 2), meaning the experimental manipulation was not fully convincing for a meaningful portion of the sample. 
Therefore, caution is required when interpreting the congruency variable effects, particularly given that the wording used to compute this variable differed between the two studies, which makes a direct comparison between them less clear.

Secondly, several of the questionnaire subscales used in this study showed low reliability, most notably the BAIT Images subscale in Study 1 and the revised COPS in Study 2 following removal of an item. 
These values fall below conventional thresholds for acceptable internal consistency, and conclusions drawn from analyses involving these specific subscales should therefore be interpreted with caution, as the items may not be capturing a coherent underlying construct.
Thirdly, individual-level estimates of sensitivity to the AI-generated label showed minimal reliable variability between participants, meaning the planned individual-differences analyses as predictors of this sensitivity could not be meaningfully tested. 
The weak correlations observed should not be interpreted as evidence that these factors play no role, but rather as uninformative given the absence of reliable variance for them to explain. 
Future studies seeking to test these hypotheses would benefit from designs or manipulations that more reliably elicit individual variability in sensitivity to the AI label.

Fourthly, while the present findings are consistent with a crossover effect linking arousal and perceived authenticity, the design cannot establish the direction of this relationship. 
As discussed above, the observed congruency effects are equally consistent with authenticity impressions shaping subsequent arousal, or with arousal being retrospectively used to infer authenticity. 
Resolving this ambiguity would require designs capable of manipulating, rather than only measuring, one of these two processes.

**[IMPLICATIONS]**


# References

<!-- References will auto-populate in the refs div below -->

::: {#refs}
:::
